coredata.dc:title: A recyclable photocatalytic tea-bag-like device model based on ultrathin Bi/C/BiOX (X = Cl, Br) nanosheets
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Haibo
Long, Bei
Ye, Kai-Hang
Cai, Yipei
He, Xinyi
Lan, Yueqi
Yang, Zujin
Ji, Hongbing
coredata.dc:description: Photocatalysis is considered as a promising method to solve the global water contamination crisis. However, the recycle of powdery photocatalysts is difficult to achieve, which limits their practical application. In this work, a recyclable photocatalytic tea-bag-like device model is proposed for the first time as far as we know, and applied in wastewater treatment. Meanwhile, a novel and efficient photocatalyst, the ultrathin BiOX (X = Cl, Br) nanosheets decorated with metal Bi, carbon and oxygen vacancy (Bi/C/BiOX), is prepared by a facile hydrolysis method. The decoration of Bi, carbon and oxygen vacancy vastly improve the optical response in visible region. Further, the introduction of oxygen vacancy level, surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect of Bi and outstanding electronic conductivity of carbon synergistically accelerate the charge separation of BiOX. Moreover, the ultrathin nanosheet structure equips Bi/C/BiOX with tremendous specific surface area and abundant active sites. Thus, the Bi/C/BiOX photocatalysts exhibit excellent adsorption capacity and photocatalytic activity for the degradation of antibiotic tetracycline hydrochloride and rhodamine B. More importantly, a considerable photocatalytic performance of the tea-bag-like device model with Bi/C/BiOX is observed and there is no leakage of photocatalysts during the photocatalytic test, demonstrating its great potential for practical application in wastewater treatment. Therefore, this work not only provides a new perspective to design efficient photocatalysts, but also offers a novel strategy for photocatalysts recycling.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalysis is considered as a promising method to solve the global water contamination crisis. However, the recycle of powdery photocatalysts is difficult to achieve, which limits their practical application. In this work, a recyclable photocatalytic tea-bag-like device model is proposed for the first time as far as we know, and applied in wastewater treatment. Meanwhile, a novel and efficient photocatalyst, the ultrathin BiOX (X = Cl, Br) nanosheets decorated with metal Bi, carbon and oxygen vacancy (Bi/C/BiOX), is prepared by a facile hydrolysis method. The decoration of Bi, carbon and oxygen vacancy vastly improve the optical response in visible region. Further, the introduction of oxygen vacancy level, surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect of Bi and outstanding electronic conductivity of carbon synergistically accelerate the charge separation of BiOX. Moreover, the ultrathin nanosheet structure equips Bi/C/BiOX with tremendous specific surface area and abundant active sites. Thus, the Bi/C/BiOX photocatalysts exhibit excellent adsorption capacity and photocatalytic activity for the degradation of antibiotic tetracycline hydrochloride and rhodamine B. More importantly, a considerable photocatalytic performance of the tea-bag-like device model with Bi/C/BiOX is observed and there is no leakage of photocatalysts during the photocatalytic test, demonstrating its great potential for practical application in wastewater treatment. Therefore, this work not only provides a new perspective to design efficient photocatalysts, but also offers a novel strategy for photocatalysts recycling.

ce:section.ce:para: For the past decades, the rapid development of society has caused worsening global problems, such as fossil energy exhaustion, environmental pollution and ecological deterioration [1–3]. Among them, water pollution, resulting from toxic organic pollutants, has resulted in severe damage on ecological environment construction and sustainable development of human society. As one of the advanced oxidation processes (AOPs), photodegradation is considered as a promising way to solve water pollution [4,5].

Bismuth oxyhalides (BiOX, X = Cl, Br) have been widely studied by researchers due to their special structure and impressive photocatalytic activity. They possess a unique layered structure which is composed of a [Bi2O2]2+ layer and two halogen ions layers and is beneficial for the separation of photoinduced carriers [6]. Their relatively positive valence band position prompts the generation of photoinduced holes (h+) with high oxidizing ability [7,8]. However, BiOX (X = Cl, Br) suffer from poor light harvesting ability due to the large band gap energy and unsatisfactory charge transfer efficiency because of the poor conductivity. Aiming at these problems, many effective strategies have been carried out, such as fabricating heterojunction [9,10], elemental doping [11,12], introducing oxygen vacancy [13,14], and incorporating with metals or carbon materials [15,16]. Compared with the noble metals such as Au, Pd, or Ag, non-noble metal bismuth (Bi) has the advantages of low cost, easy availability and facile synthesis [17]. And as a semimetal, bismuth has a very small band overlap, and possesses a highly anisotropic Fermi surface, low carrier density, low carrier effective mass, high carrier motilities, and long carrier mean free path [18]. Moreover, Bi nanoparticles have been found to exhibit surface plasmon resonance (SPR) properties, which could enhance the light absorption and charge separation of photocatalysts ascribed to the excitation of electromagnetic field [19]. Therefore, metal Bi was employed by researchers to enhance the performance of photocatalysts, such as Bi/BiOCl [17], Bi/BiOBr x I1-
x
[18], Bi/BiOI/(BiO)2CO3 [19], Bi/Bi2O3 [20], Bi/g-C3N4 [21], Bi/SiO2 [22] and Bi/Fe0 [23]. On the other hand, carbon materials such as graphene, graphene oxide (GO), carbon nanofiber, and carbon dots/nanodots/quantum dots (CDs/CNDs/CQDs), have attracted considerable attention due to their outstanding optical and electrical property, which could simultaneously enlarge the light adsorption region and decrease the recombination of photoinduced carriers of photocatalysts [24,25]. Thus, carbon materials are widely adopted to modify BiOX photocatalyst, such as graphene/BiOCl [26], GO/BiOCl [27], GO/polyaniline/BiOBr [28], CDs/BiOI [29], GO/CNDs/BiOBr [30] and CQDs/BiOX (X = Br, Cl) [31]. But combinative modification of carbon and Bi for photocatalyst is rarely reported and may exhibit a further improvement. Moreover, the introduction of oxygen vacancies is widely used for the improvement of photocatalyst. Oxygen vacancies formed on the open interfaces could enhance light absorption and charge transport, further improve the photocatalytic performance [13,32]. And it was reported that in the ultrathin nanosheets, the buried internal oxygen atoms become easier to escape, making formation of oxygen vacancies, such as ultrathin BiOCl [33] and ultrathin Bi2MoO6 [34]. Apart from the above modification methods, the increased specific surface area is also an important factor for photocatalysis. Especially when it comes to practical application, admirable adsorption capacity can hinder the pollutant diffusion and shorten the wastewater treatment time [35]. Fabricating ultrathin nanosheet structure is an efficient way to increase specific surface area.

As photocatalysts developed by the present researchers are mostly in form of powder, the separation and recycle of photocatalysts from water become another big challenge to the application of photocatalysis [36]. Moreover, the powdery photocatalyst may lead to secondary pollution to natural water unless they are recycled [37]. To solve this problem, several ways have been proposed at present. Using or combining the magnetic photocatalysts is an efficient way to fabricate the recyclable photocatalysts, such as Fe3O4 [37,38], CoFe2O4 [25,39], ZnFe2O4 [40]. But this method has severe limitation which can only be applicable to those few magnetic photocatalysts. Another way is to grow or immobilize photocatalysts on substrates, such as activated carbon fibers [41,42], Ti substrates [43], and Cu nets [36]. However, most of these substrates are quite expensive and even exceed the cost of photocatalysts. Thus, the development of a simple and cost-effective way to recycle photocatalyst is still challenging.

In this work, a facile hydrolysis method is proposed to synthesize the ultrathin BiOX (X = Cl, Br) nanosheets decorated with metal Bi, carbon material and oxygen vacancy (Bi/C/BiOX). The light absorption and charge separation capacity of BiOX nanosheets are noticeably enhanced after decorating with metal Bi, carbon and oxygen vacancy. And the specific surface areas of the catalysts substantially increase due to the ultrathin nanosheet structure, which results in their wonderful adsorptive ability. Hence, the Bi/C/BiOX photocatalysts exhibit outstanding performance on the removal of antibiotic tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH) and rhodamine B (RhB). Meanwhile, a recyclable photocatalytic tea-bag-like device model is proposed and fabricated successfully for the first time as far as we know. This tea-bag-like device is suitable to contain and rapidly replace all kinds of powdery photocatalysts. The considerable photocatalytic performance confirms the potential application of this recyclable device model. Therefore, this work not only provides new insight into the design of photocatalysts, but also offers an effective strategy to solve the recycling problem of powdery photocatalyst.
All reagents used in this work were analytical grade and purchased from commercial corporations without any further purification, and deionized water was used through all the experiments.

Bi/C/Bi2O3 nanosphere precursor was synthesized by a solvothermal method followed by a calcination with Ar protection. In a typical experimental procedure, 1.5 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was dissolved in 20 mL 1 mol L−1 HNO3 under stirring, then 0.12 mmol Polyvinyl Pyrrolidone (PVP) and 100 mL ethylene glycol (EG) were added and dissolved into the solution. The mixture was poured into 40 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclaves and maintained at 150 °C for 12 h. The products were centrifuged at 9000 r/min for 10 min and washed with water and alcohol for 3 times respectively. After dried at 60 °C for 12 h, the products were calcined at 500 °C for 1 h in Ar airflow. During the calcination process, EG and PVP underwent the carbonization process under high temperature. Metal Bi was formed by the carbothermal reduction reaction. Eventually, Bi/C/Bi2O3 nanosphere precursor was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Bi/C/BiOX (X = Cl, Br) nanosheets and BiOX microspheres
ce:section.ce:para: 0.1 g of Bi/C/Bi2O3 nanosphere precursor was added into 10 mL of 1 mol L−1 HX (X = Cl, Br) solution. This suspension liquid was ultrasonicated and violently stirred for 30 min respectively to make sure that all Bi2O3 was dissolved, and metal Bi and carbon remained good dispersity. After that, 20 mL of 2% ammonia solution was quickly dropwise added into the mixture under stirring. The BiOX nanosheets were formed by hydrolysis and decorated with metal Bi and carbon. The Bi/C/BiOX nanosheet composites were collected by centrifugation, washed with water and alcohol for 3 times respectively, and dried.

As the contrastive photocatalysts, the common BiOX microspheres were synthesized by a frequently-used solvothermal method [44]. In details, 20 mL methanol containing 1 mmol KX (X = Cl, Br) was mixed with 20 mL glycol dissolving 1 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O. After stirred for 2 h, the mixture solution was placed in a dried Teflon-lined stainless autoclaves and kept at 160 °C for 8 h. The product was collected, washed, and dried.

The crystal phases and composition of the samples were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) with Cu Kα radiation (D-MAX 2200 VPC, Rigaku, Japan), Raman Spectrometer with 514.5 nm laser (inVia, Renishaw, UK), and Thermogravimetry (TG) analysis from 30 to 800 °C with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 under air atmosphere (TG209 F1, Netzsch, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Nexsa, Thermo Fisher, UK) was employed to further verify the composition of the samples. The scanning electron microscopy (SEM, G500, Zeiss, Germany) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 F30, FEI, US) were used to characterize the morphology, microstructure and element distribution of the samples. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were collected by a SPM-9500 J3 microscope (Bruker, Germany). The specific surface area of the samples was recorded on an ASAP 2020 V3.03H instrument and analyzed by the multipoint Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method. The light absorption spectra were measured by UV–vis-NIR spectrophotometer (UV 2450, Shimadzu, Japan), while photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FLSP920, Edinburg, UK). The electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) and electron spin resonance (ESR) tests were carried out on an EPR spectrometer (A300-10-12, Bruker, Germany) at room temperature. The intermediates in the photodegradation of TCH (taking the solution of Bi/C/BiOCl after 15 min’ irradiation as the sample) was analyzed by a high resolution liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HRLC-MS, UltiMate3000-timsTOF, Bruker, Germany) in electrospray positive ion mode (ESI+, capillary voltage 3 kV) equipped with a C18 column (Hypersil GOLD C18, 2.1 mm × 100 mm, 1.9 μm). The mobile phase was a mixture of formic acid (0.1%) and acetonitrile in a multi-step gradient with a flow rate of 0.3 mL min−1, and the column temperature was 40 °C.

The photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of the samples were performed in a standard three-electrode cell using 0.1 mol L−1 KH2PO4 solution as electrolyte on a CHI 760E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, Shanghai) with visible illumination from an 1.5 G solar simulator (LCS 100 94011A, Newport, US), where an Ag/AgCl electrode was employed as the reference electrode, the Pt wires worked as the counter electrode, and the FTO coated with the samples acted as the working electrodes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic activity and adsorptive ability tests
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic activity tests were investigated by the degradation of TCH and RhB under the visible light of a 300 W Xe lamp (100 mW cm−2 light intensity) with a 420 nm UV light cut filter and circulating cooling water at 25 °C. Specifically, 20 mg photocatalyst was added into 100 mL 10 mg L−1 TCH or RhB solution and stirred in dark for 1 h to reach the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. After turning on the light, 3 mL of the reactive solution was drawn out every 15 or 5 min. After centrifugation, the residual TCH or RhB was detected by a UV–vis spectrophotometer at absorption wavelength of 357 nm or 554 nm, respectively. Similarly, a photocatalytic activity experiment of RhB without the adsorption process was carried out, which means turning on the light immediately after adding the photocatalyst. Besides, the adsorptive ability test of RhB (10 mg L−1) over the samples was performed under dark, and the sorption behavior of Bi/C/BiOCl nanosheets was evaluated in the solutions with different initial concentrations of RhB (5, 10, 20, 40, 80 mg L−1, respectively).
The photocatalytic performance of the tea-bag-like device model was tested in similar way over RhB but using 50 mg photocatalyst, stirring in dark for 1 h and sampling every 30 min.

The initial nanospheres precursor with a diameter of about 100 nm (Fig. S1) was synthesized by a solvothermal method. The preparation process of Bi/C/BiOX (X = Cl, Br) nanosheet composites is illustrated in Fig. 1 a. During the calcination process, carbon is formed by the carbonization of PVP and EG under high temperature, and a part of Bi3+ are reduced by the carbothermal reduction reaction, but most of Bi3+ turn into Bi2O3. Eventually, the initial nanospheres change into Bi/C/Bi2O3 nanosphere precursor. Then, diluted HX (X = Cl, Br) solution is added to dissolve Bi2O3 with assistance of ultrasonication and stirring, but metal Bi is remained due to its chemical stability within non-oxidizing acids such as diluted HX. Finally, BiOX nanosheet composites decorated with metal Bi and carbon were fabricated by hydrolysis after dropwise adding ammonia solution. The proposed chemical equations can be explained as follows:
(1) Bi2O3 + 6HX = 2BiX3 + 3H2O
(2) BiX3 + 2NH3·H2O = BiOX + 2NH4X + H2O

Fig. 1b shows the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the as-prepared samples. No obvious peak is detected for the initial nanospheres precursor. After calcination, the clear peaks appear for Bi/C/Bi2O3. The peaks located at 27.9°, 31.7°, 32.7°, 46.0° and 55.5° can be assigned to the tetragonal Bi2O3 (JCPDS No. 27-0050), while the peaks at 27.2°, 37.9° and 39.6° are attributed to the generation of rhombohedral Bi (JCPDS No. 44-1246). As the contrastive samples, BiOX (X = Cl, Br) are well identical to the standard tetragonal structure BiOCl (JCPDS No. 06-0249) and BiOBr (JCPDS No. 09-0393), respectively. For Bi/C/BiOX samples, except the main peaks from BiOX, several weak peaks at 27.2°, 37.9° and 39.6° appear, which are indexed to metal Bi. A partial enlarged view (25°–29°) of the patterns is displayed left beside Fig. 1b, which shows the peak of metal Bi at 27.2° in Bi/C/BiOX more clearly. Additionally, the peak broadening of Bi/C/BiOX is observed compared to pure BiOX, implying the smaller crystal size of Bi/C/BiOX [45].

Raman spectra were collected to further verify the sample composition. Obviously, two characteristic peaks at about 1361 and 1588 cm−1 are observed for Bi/C/Bi2O3 and Bi/C/BiOX samples in Fig. 1c, which correspond to the D and G bands of carbon and confirm the existence of carbon [46]. Fig. 1d displays the Raman spectra at low wave number. For Bi/C/Bi2O3 sample, peaks at around 115, 126, 137 and 150 cm−1 are attributed to the bond vibrations of Bi2O3 [47]. Additionally, two characteristic peaks at 69 and 94 cm−1 of metal bismuth are identified, representing the E g and A 1g Raman bonds [48]. Besides, the peaks located at 59, 143 and 199 cm−1 for BiOCl are assigned to the A 1g external, A 1g and E g internal Bi-Cl stretching modes respectively [49]. Likewise, the Bi-Br stretching modes of BiOBr are located at 56, 113 and 159 cm−1 [50]. Notably, an additional peak at 97 cm−1 comes up in the spectrum of Bi/C/BiOCl, which not only manifests the existence of Bi but also implies the strong electronic interaction between metal Bi and BiOCl, judging from the peak shift [51]. For the same reason, the peaks shift of BiOX in Bi/C/BiOX composites can be observed, as depicted in Fig. 1d. It is difficult to recognize the Raman peak of Bi in Bi/C/BiOBr due to the peak overlap between BiOBr and Bi. In addition, according to the results of thermogravimetry (TG) analysis (Fig. S2), carbon contents are about 3.7% for Bi/C/BiOCl and 3.1% for Bi/C/BiOBr. More details of TG analysis are available in Supporting Information (SI). For better comparison, hydrolytic BiOX, C/BiOX and Bi/BiOX are prepared. Their preparation method, XRD patterns (Fig. S3) and Raman spectra (Fig. S4) can be seen in SI.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to further verify the composition of Bi/C/BiOX samples. As shown in Fig. S5a and c, only C, O, Bi, Cl or Br elements are detected in the survey, indicating the high purity of Bi/C/BiOX samples. In Fig. 2 a and d, both samples exhibit the CC (284.7 eV), CO (286.0 eV) and CO (288.5 eV) peaks at C 1s XPS spectra, demonstrating the composition of carbon [28,52]. Fig. S5b and d exhibit the Cl 2p and Br 3d XPS spectra, which are in correspondence with the Cl 2p1/2 and Cl 2p3/2 of Cl in BiOCl, Br 3d3/2 and Br 3d5/2 of Br in BiOBr, respectively. As for O 1s XPS spectra (Fig. 2b and e), both samples display the lattice oxygen at 530.3 eV, assigning to the Bi-O bond in the crystal structure of [Bi2O2]2+, and the peaks at 531.6 or 531.5 eV are contributed to oxygen vacancies on the surface [53–55]. The ultrathin nanosheet structure prompts the generation of oxygen vacancies [33,34]. The presence of oxygen vacancies was further investigated by electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectrometer. The typical character of oxygen vacancy and the signal of Bi0 are collected at around g = 2.005, more details are provided in Fig. S6. For the XPS spectra of Bi 4f (Fig. 2e and j), besides the signals of Bi3+ in [Bi2O2]2+ (159.5 and 164.8 eV), the signals of Bi0 (157.4 and 162.7 eV) are clearly detected, further confirming the decoration of metal Bi in Bi/C/BiOX samples [19,20].

To shed light on the morphology and element distribution of the samples, scanning electron microscopy (SEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were employed, as shown in Fig. 3 and Figs. S7–S9. The contrastive BiOX samples are microspheres with a diameter of about 2 μm, composed by numerous nanosheets with a thickness of about 30 nm for BiOCl and 40 nm for BiOBr (Fig. S7). Prepared by hydrolysis, the Bi/C/BiOX samples are made up of plentiful interlaced nanosheets (Fig. 3a and i), which are different from the pure hydrolytic BiOX (Fig. S8). The nanosheets of pure hydrolytic BiOX tend to assemble into microflowers. Meanwhile, about 5 nm thick nanosheets with flat surface are observed by AFM for Bi/C/BiOX (Fig. 3b and j). The TEM images further confirm the interlaced nanosheet morphology (Fig. S9). The ultrathin nanosheet morphology is highly beneficial for the photocatalytic performance, which can vastly enlarge the specific surface area, enhance the adsorption capacity and provide more active sites [56]. Moreover, well-defined crystal lattices can be observed in the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (Fig. 3c and k), revealing their high crystallinity. From the inset of Fig. 3c, the explicit lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.275 nm belong to the (1 1 0) atomic planes of BiOCl [57], while the lattice fringes with 0.282 nm interplanar spacing in the inset of Fig. 3k are assigned to the (1 0 2) atomic planes of BiOBr [58].

It is worth noting that some dots with distinctly different lattice fringes appear in HRTEM images of Bi/C/BiOCl, which are circled with yellow lines. From the enlarged view (right beside Fig. 3c) of the dot in Fig. 3c, the clear lattice fringes with the interplanar spacing of 0.328 nm are highly consistent with the spacing of (0 1 2) atomic planes of metal Bi [20]. Such dot with different spacing of 0.395 nm is also found in Bi/C/BiOBr (Fig. 3k), which is in accordance with the (0 0 3) atomic planes of metal Bi (right beside Fig. 3k). These results further demonstrate the decoration of metal Bi over the nanosheets. However, carbon in Bi/C/BiOX cannot be found, resulting from its high dispersity and amorphous property. To make an inquiry into the composition and distribution of the elements in the composites, the Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS) elemental mappings were collected. As exhibited in Fig. 3d-h and l-p, Bi (red), O (blue), Cl/Br (yellow) and C (green) elements are uniformly distributed on the nanosheets. EDS mappings acquired by SEM further confirm the uniform elemental distribution (Fig. S10).

Compared to the BiOX, the visible change of Bi/C/BiOX is their darker color after decorated with metal Bi and carbon (insets of Fig. 4 a), indicating their better light absorbing ability. Fig. 4a shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of the samples. Close light absorption edges are detected between BiOX and the corresponding Bi/C/BiOX samples, but Bi/C/BiOX can effectively absorb the visible light which BiOX can barely utilize. Generally, the optical band gap of photocatalyst can be calculated by the typical Tauc method by employing the following equation [59]:
(3)
α h ν = A
h ν -
E g
n / 2
where α, h, ν, A, and Eg are the absorption coefficient, Plank’s constant, light frequency, a constant, and band gap. The value of n here is 4 because BiOX (X = Cl, Br) are indirect transition semiconductors [53]. Therefore, as demonstrated in Fig. 4b, the band gaps of the photocatalysts are estimated to be 3.17, 3.03, 2.76 and 2.34 eV for BiOCl, Bi/C/BiOCl, BiOBr and Bi/C/BiOBr, respectively. The absorption over the whole visible light region and the narrowed band gaps should be induced by the synergistic effect of the decoration of carbon, the SPR effects of metal Bi and the presence of oxygen vacancies [17,60,61]. And the UV–visible absorption spectra of Bi/BiOX and C/BiOX are also provided in Fig. S11 for better comparison, which indicate that both metal Bi and carbon alone can enhance light absorption property of BiOX. When they together decorate on BiOX, Bi/C/BiOX display a further increment and the best light absorption property. More details are provided in SI.

Owing to the outstanding electron attracting, trapping and transferring ability of metal Bi and carbon, their decoration can strengthen the conductivity and charge separation ability of BiOX nanosheets [29,62]. The transient photocurrent responses were carried out, which reveal the charge separation and migration of the photocatalyst, as shown in Fig. 5 a [63]. When lighting on, the photocurrents of Bi/C/BiOX are evidently higher than those of BiOX, and Bi/C/BiOCl possesses the strongest photocurrent. These results demonstrate that the better charge separation efficiency and conductivity are achieved after decorating with Bi and carbon, especially for Bi/C/BiOCl sample.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is recorded to assess the interfacial charge transport property of photocatalysts. It is widely accepted that the smaller arc radius of the curves stands for the lower charge transfer resistance [64]. As presented in Fig. 5b, the curves under light irradiation possess smaller arc radiuses than their corresponding curves in dark due to the photoelectric effect. Notably, the arc radiuses of Bi/C/BiOX curves are dramatically smaller than those of BiOX no matter in dark or under light, implying the acceleration effect of metal Bi, carbon and oxygen vacancy on charge transport. In addition, Bi/C/BiOCl has the smallest arc radiuses, in accordance with the result of photocurrent, suggesting that it may have the best photocatalytic activity.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra are applied to evaluate the separation efficiency of charge carriers [65]. In Fig. 5c and d, the PL intensity of Bi/C/BiOX visibly declines in comparison to that of BiOX. The depressed PL emission is benefited from the trapping of photoelectrons by oxygen vacancies, metal Bi and carbon.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specific surface area and adsorption capacity
ce:section.ce:para: Specific surface area mostly determines the rate of pollutant adsorption during photocatalytic process and is related to the number of active sites, thus it is significant to photocatalysts [34]. Fig. 6 a shows the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of the samples. All the isotherms can be classified to type IV isotherm, revealing the typical mesoporous characteristics of the samples [66]. The specific surface areas of Bi/C/BiOCl and Bi/C/BiOBr are 57.8 and 47.7 m2 g−1 respectively, which are much larger than those of BiOCl (19.3 m2 g−1) and BiOBr (10.2 m2 g−1), as listed in Table 1 . Benefiting from the ultrathin nanosheet structure, the pore volumes of Bi/C/BiOX are obviously increased, and their average pore size are smaller, as illustrated in Fig. 6b and Table 1.

To investigate the effect of specific surface area on adsorption capacity, the RhB adsorption tests were carried out in dark. As shown in Fig. 6c, all the catalysts exhibit a quick adsorption within the first 5 min, then the adsorption process slows down and finally reaches the adsorption-desorption equilibrium after stirring for 40 min. Notably, the adsorption performance of Bi/C/BiOCl (48%) and Bi/C/BiOBr (43%) are about 3 or 4 times higher than those of BiOCl (12%) and BiOBr (8%) (Table 1). The tremendously elevated adsorption performance confirms the advantage of the larger specific surface area, which is beneficial to the fast elimination of pollutants. In addition, the sorption kinetics and isotherms of RhB on Bi/C/BiOCl nanosheets with difference initial concentrations were evaluated, the results are shown in Fig. S12 and Table S1, S2 respectively. The good correlation coefficients (R2 > 0.99) for the pseudo-second-order kinetic model imply that chemical adsorption could possibly occur during the adsorption process. Further, the equilibrium sorption data are satisfactorily fitted with Freundlich model instead of Langmuir model, which indicates a stronger adsorbate-adsorbent interaction is formed. Detailed descriptions are available in SI.

To probe the practical photocatalytic performance of Bi/C/BiOX nanosheets, the degradation of RhB and TCH solution was performed under visible light irradiation. The blank experiment indicates that RhB is barely degraded under illumination without photocatalysts (Fig. 7 a). Before turning on the light, the mixture was stirred in dark for 1 h to reach the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. When lighting up, Bi/C/BiOX photocatalysts can promptly decompose RhB while BiOX are much slow. Specifically, Bi/C/BiOCl exhibits 96.8% degradation ratio of RhB within 10 min irradiation, and Bi/C/BiOBr possesses 78.0% degradation ratio. But BiOCl and BiOBr could only eliminate 42.0% and 18.5% RhB within the same time. Among them, Bi/C/BiOCl possesses the best photocatalytic activity, which is coincided with the previous analyses. To demonstrate the photocatalytic activities more concretely, the corresponding pseudo-first-order reaction kinetics are supplied in Fig. 7b. After fitting, the first order rate constants of Bi/C/BiOCl and Bi/C/BiOBr are calculated to be 0.2280 and 0.1269 min−1, which are more than 3 and 10 times higher than those of BiOCl (0.0552 min−1) and BiOBr (0.0112 min−1), respectively. The photocatalytic performances of carbon, hydrolytic BiOX, Bi/BiOX and C/BiOX samples are also discussed in Figs. S13 and S14 for better comparison.

It is well-known that dye molecules like RhB usually have photosensitization process which is beneficial to their degradation, especially for the photocatalysts with wild band gap energy [67,68]. As a contrast, TCH is an achromatous molecule with negligible photosensitization under visible light. Further, TCH is an important and widely used antibiotic but becomes a resistant pharmaceutical residue broadly distributed in wastewater or environment nowadays. As a result, TCH leads to severe negative impact and needs to be eliminated [69]. Thus, the degradation of TCH was carried out to examine the photocatalytic activities of Bi/C/BiOX samples as well. Fig. 7c depicts the degradation efficiency of TCH, and Fig. S15 displays their corresponding UV–vis absorption spectra. As can be seen, TCH is barely decomposed without photocatalysts under visible light irradiation. When adding photocatalysts, Bi/C/BiOX have admirable adsorption performance of TCH after stirring in dark. After turning on the light, the concentration of TCH rapidly decreases in the first 15 min, and Bi/C/BiOCl has the wonderful removal ratio of 80.0%. Within the same time, Bi/C/BiOBr can degrade 56.3% of TCH, BiOCl and BiOBr can only remove 45.3% and 24.0% of TCH respectively. The Bi/C/BiOX samples still have much better photocatalytic activities compared to BiOX samples, owing to the superior adsorptive and photocatalytic capacity. Fig. 7d is the corresponding pseudo-first-order reaction kinetics and the first order rate constants, where Bi/C/BiOCl and Bi/C/BiOBr are 0.0420 and 0.0200 min−1, about 3 times as many as BiOCl (0.0149 min−1) and BiOBr (0.0112 min−1) respectively. Though without the photosensitization process, the Bi/C/BiOX samples still show considerable photocatalytic activities for the degradation of TCH, revealing their potential application on other emerging contaminants than dyes.

Usually, the photodegradation tests are performed after reaching the adsorption-desorption equilibrium by stirring in dark for a certain time, but it is wasteful from the practical standpoint. On the other hand, in some emergencies like the leakage of organic chemicals to natural water, it particularly requires a catalyst which can both adsorb and degrade the pollutants rapidly thus prevent the pollutant dispersion as much as possible. Hence, the photodegradation of RhB was also carried out without stirring in dark to evaluate the rapid degradation ability of photocatalysts. As shown in Fig. 7e, the Bi/C/BiOX still have a quick degradation of RhB. For example, Bi/C/BiOCl thoroughly removes the pollutant in 20 min, which is slightly slower than the previous test (15 min). 5 more minutes of irradiation is needed to achieve the same performance, but 1 h of stirring in dark is saved. Impressively, the first order rate constants of Bi/C/BiOCl still maintain at a high level of 0.17858 min−1 (Fig. 7f). This result suggests that, with excellent adsorptive property and photocatalytic activity, Bi/C/BiOX photocatalysts (especially Bi/C/BiOCl) are suitable for rapid elimination of organic pollutants in emergencies. Furthermore, the results of cyclic test prove that Bi/C/BiOCl has outstanding reusability, high stability of morphology and structure under visible light (Fig. S16), indicating its promising practical application. More details are provided in SI.

The intermediates in the photodegradation of TCH were identified by HRLC-MS, the result and the corresponding analysis are displayed in Fig. 8 . In Fig. 8a, the mass peak at m/z = 445 corresponds to the residual TC, the hydrolysate of TCH, and the main mass peaks at m/z = 437, 417, 337, 279, 263, 188, 118 and so on correspond to the intermediates. According to the m/z values and the possible molecular structure of the intermediates, it can be inferred that TC molecules are attacked by the reactive species, and go through demethylation, deamination, dehydroxylation and ring open reaction, then gradually are degraded to lower molecular weight organic compounds (alkanolamine, phenolic compound, etc.), and finally turn into low molecular weight organic acids (phenylacetic acid, hexanedioic acid, butanedioic acid, etc.). Based on the results and literatures [69–72], the possible photodegradation pathways of TCH are proposed in Fig. 8b.

The recycle of photocatalyst is of great significance to its practical application. In this work, a recyclable photocatalytic tea-bag-like device is proposed and successfully fabricated for the first time as far as we know. As demonstrated in Fig. 9 a and b, the device model is made up of a transparent plastic zip lock bag and a permeable membrane (MCE-0.45 μm, JINTENG, China), which can ensure the transmission of light and pollution simultaneously. The zip lock bag enables the easy and rapid padding as well as the replacement of photocatalysts. This device can float on the solution (as shown in Fig. 9c), hence the recycle of photocatalyst and the device is convenient. Meanwhile, the device is low-cost and easy to manufacture, which is suitable for mass production. As a result, such device is very beneficial to the practical application of powdery photocatalysts, especially for those expensive photocatalysts and to prevent the secondary pollution during the pollution treatment.

To intuitively evaluate the performance of the device model in practical use, the photodegradation of RhB with Bi/C/BiOCl photocatalyst inside it is performed, and the photographs are displayed in Fig. 9c. The concentration gradient of RhB between the inside and the outside of device is the main driving force for mass transfer. RhB is removed gradually and there is no leakage of photocatalyst into the solution. After 5 h irradiation, Bi/C/BiOCl exhibits the best performance with 85% degradation ratio, as shown in Fig. 9d. It is obvious that the photocatalytic performances of catalysts in devices are lower than those without devices. This is mainly due to the low mass transfer rate. Therefore, seeking a membrane with excellent mass transfer capability or supplying a strong driving force for mass transfer are the key to improve the performance of our device and meet the demand of practical application. Meanwhile, the further improvements of the device are very important, such as the transmission of light of zip lock bag and the dispersity of photocatalyst.

To clarify the specific reactive species produced during the photocatalytic process and explore the possible photocatalytic mechanism, the degradation of RhB over Bi/C/BiOCl was carried out by adding different scavengers. In detail, 1 mmol of tert-butylalcohol (TBA, hydroxyl radical (
OH) scavenger), benzoquinone (BQ, superoxide radical (
O2 −) scavenger) and triethanolamine (TEOA, photoinduced holes (h+) scavenger) were added into the solution before turning on the light, respectively. Fig. 10 a shows that the addition of TBA barely affects the photocatalytic process, indicating that
OH is not contributed to the photodegradation. The addition of BQ causes a slight depression of performance, suggesting that
O2 − plays a role in degradation process. However, the photocatalytic performance is seriously repressed in presence of TEOA, demonstrating that h+ is the primary reactive species and plays the dominant role in degradation process.

To further confirm the above result, electron spin resonance (ESR) spin-trap measurements were carried out to explore the production of
OH and
O2 − radicals under irradiation with the assistance of radical scavenger, 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO). Remarkably, the strong signals are recorded for Bi/C/BiOCl in dark, as shown in Fig. 10b and c. ESR is usually used to detect the unpaired electrons, thus these intensive signals should originate from the single electrons in the 6p atomic orbital of metal Bi (electron configuration of Bi is [Xe] 4f14 5d10 6s2 6p3) [20]. Despite of the ESR signal of metal Bi, a negligible change is observed in the spectra of DMPO-
OH after 5 min irradiation for the samples (Fig. 10b), indicating that few
OH is generated. Nevertheless, in Fig. 10c, a clear quartet signal with an intensity ratio of 1:1:1:1 appears for BiOCl after lighting, which is assigned to the characteristic signal of DMPO-
O2 − adduct, revealing the generation of
O2 − [73]. The stronger signal is collected for Bi/C/BiOCl after lighting, implying the generation of more
O2 − reactive species. Same reactive species detection and ESR results are obtained for BiOBr and Bi/C/BiOBr, as exhibited in Fig. S17.

Based on the above experiments and analyses, the photocatalytic mechanism of Bi/C/BiOX nanocomposites is disclosed, as depicted in Fig. 11 . On the one hand, the ultrathin nanosheet structure provides the photocatalysts with considerable specific surface area, which greatly increases their adsorptive property. On the other hand, the decoration of metal Bi and carbon and the presence of oxygen vacancies equip Bi/C/BiOX with outstanding light harvest ability and excellent photoinduced electron separation and transfer efficiency. Under visible light irradiation, the electrons on the valence band (VB) of BiOX are excited to oxygen vacancy levels, which is also aided by the electromagnetic field created by metal Bi [55]. Also, electrons and holes can be excited over Bi nanoparticles due to their SPR effects under visible light irradiation. When Bi nanoparticles combine with BiOX, forming an intimate interfacial contact of Bi and BiOX, the photoinduced holes over metal Bi can recombine with electrons from the oxygen vacancy levels of BiOX via a Z-scheme process [5,17]. Moreover, the electrons also can be quickly transferred to carbon material through the contact interface owing to their outstanding electronic conductivity and electronic storage feature[30]. Thus, BiOX nanosheets are able to capture and utilize more light to generate more photoelectrons. Then the photoelectrons are trapped and delivered by metal Bi and carbon to react with O2 in the solution to form
O2 −, effectively improving the separation of photoinduced charge carriers. Meanwhile, the extensive holes (h+) in the valence band with strong oxidizing property play the leading role to directly mineralize the pollutants. In despite of the disadvantage of light harvest ability, Bi/C/BiOCl still shows better performance than Bi/C/BiOBr, which could be attributed to its superior adsorption capacity, charge separation efficiency and interfacial charge transport property. Meanwhile, the higher valence band maximum (VBM) energy of Bi/C/BiOCl (Fig. S18) might also lead to its better performance by producing h+ with stronger oxidizing property.

In summary, the ultrathin BiOX nanosheets decorated with metal Bi, carbon and oxygen vacancy (Bi/C/BiOX) are successfully synthesized by a facile hydrolysis method. The Bi/C/BiOX photocatalyst exhibits excellent adsorption capacity and photocatalytic ability, which can thoroughly eliminate RhB after 15 min irradiation with adsorption-desorption equilibrium process. Even without the adsorption process, they still can rapidly remove the pollutant after 20 min irradiation, implying that they are suitable for the quick elimination of organic pollutants in practice. Furthermore, Bi/C/BiOX demonstrates admirable degradation ability of TCH without the auxiliary photosensitization process, declaring its promising ability to eliminate various contaminants. The improved performance of Bi/C/BiOX is demonstrated to be benefit from the increased specific surface area, light absorption ability, conductivity and charge separation efficiency. Further, the roles and enhanced mechanisms of metal Bi, carbon and oxygen vacancy are proposed and illustrated, such as SPR effects, electronic conductivity and storage, and oxygen vacancy levels, respectively. More importantly, a recyclable tea-bag-like device model is proposed and fabricated to achieve the recycle of photocatalyst. The successful application and recycle of tea-bag-like device model indicate its practical application prospect. Thus, this work may open a new avenue for the recycle of photocatalyst and facilitate the application of photocatalysis in wastewater treatment.
